title: SEGMENT 11 — Assembly, budding, and the maturation switch

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Assembly, budding, and the maturation switch
Assembly happens at the plasma membrane.

Several thousand Gag molecules — roughly 2,400 for HIV-1, with about 120 Gag-Pol molecules sprinkled in at the frameshift-determined ratio — oligomerize on the inner leaflet, latticing into a hemispherical shell that pushes the membrane outward into a budding particle.

The C-terminus of each Gag points outward, the matrix domain interacts with the membrane, the capsid and NC domains face inward, NC clutches the genomic RNA, and the trimeric Env spikes that have accumulated at the plasma membrane get incorporated into the budding particle.
To pinch the bud off — to actually scission the membrane neck and release the particle — the virus needs help.
It cannot do this alone.
It hijacks a cellular pathway called the ESCRT machinery, the endosomal sorting complex required for transport.
ESCRT is normally used by the cell to bud vesicles into multivesicular bodies and to seal off the abscission point during cytokinesis.
The geometry is exactly the same as a virus budding off the cell — membrane being pinched away from the cytoplasm — and HIV exploits ESCRT to perform that scission.
The hijack happens through small sequences in the p6 region of Gag called late domains, or L-domains.
HIV-1 p6 has a PTAP motif that binds the ESCRT-I component TSG101, and a YPXL-type motif that binds the ESCRT-associated factor ALIX.
These interactions recruit ESCRT-III and the ATPase VPS4 to the bud neck, which catalyze the membrane scission.
Mutations in the late domains produce a striking phenotype — particles that bud halfway but stay tethered to the cell by a thin membrane neck, sometimes called "frustrated budding.
" Beautiful electron microscopy images.
Useful in the lab.
Lethal for the virus.
OK.
The particle has now budded off.
It is floating in the extracellular space.
Is it infectious?
Not yet.
The freshly budded particle is called the immature virion.
The structure is a roughly spherical shell of intact Gag polyproteins, with intact Gag-Pol fusion proteins woven in.
Inside, the genomic RNA is bound by NC, which is still attached to the rest of Gag.
The capsid is not the iconic conical structure yet — it's a roughly spherical shell of uncleaved Gag.
For the particle to become infectious, it must undergo maturation.

The viral protease, which until now has been held inactive as part of the Gag-Pol polyprotein, gets activated — partly autocatalytically through dimerization — and cleaves Gag and Gag-Pol at a series of specific sites.
Roughly ten cleavage events per Gag/Gag-Pol pair.
The individual proteins — MA, CA, NC, p6, PR, RT, IN — are released.
They rearrange.
Capsid molecules reassemble into the famous conical structure, with NC and the genome packaged inside, RT and IN tucked in.
Matrix stays attached to the membrane.
Env trimers sit on the outside.
This rearrangement is dramatic and visible in cryo-EM.
Immature particle: roughly spherical Gag shell.
Mature particle: conical capsid with empty space around it.
Same molecules, completely different architecture, all triggered by protease cleavage.
If protease is inhibited, maturation does not happen.
The particle remains immature.
The conical capsid never forms.
The RT cannot perform reverse transcription efficiently in a target cell.
The particle is non-infectious.

This is the mechanism of action of every protease inhibitor in HIV therapy: they bind in the protease active site, block the cleavage of Gag and Gag-Pol, and produce immature, non-infectious particles.
We'll cover the drug classes in segment 13.
So to recap the life cycle so far: entry, reverse transcription, integration, transcription, splicing, translation, frameshift, assembly, budding, maturation.
We've covered the whole arc.
